title: Critical npm Supply Chain Attack Targets Popular Packages With 50M+ Weekly Downloads
subtitle: Malicious code injected into dependency tree — update immediately
excerpt: A coordinated supply chain attack has compromised several high-download npm packages. The malicious payload exfiltrates environment variables including API keys and tokens.
seo_title: npm Supply Chain Attack: Which Packages Are Affected and How to Respond
seo_description: A critical npm supply chain attack is actively exfiltrating environment variables. Learn which packages are compromised and how to audit your dependencies.
body:
What Happened

Security researchers at Socket.dev identified a coordinated supply chain attack targeting the npm ecosystem. The attack vector begins with account takeovers of package maintainers, followed by new patch-level releases containing obfuscated malicious code that exfiltrates process.env to an attacker-controlled server.

The exfiltrated data includes any environment variables present in the Node.js process at the time the package is loaded — which in typical CI/CD and server environments means AWS credentials, API keys, database URLs, and authentication tokens.

How to Check Your Project

npx @socket/cli audit
npm audit --audit-level=moderate

The Socket CLI provides the most complete picture. Standard npm audit will not catch this class of attack because the malicious code does not exploit a known vulnerability — it was deliberately authored and published.

Immediate Actions

If you believe you are affected:

  1. Rotate all secrets in your environment immediately — treat them as compromised
  2. Audit CI/CD pipeline logs for outbound connections to unfamiliar endpoints
  3. Pin your dependency versions and avoid automatic patch updates until you've audited your full tree
  4. Enable npm provenance checks where available

The Broader Problem

This is not the first npm supply chain attack and will not be the last. The registry's security model relies heavily on maintainer account security, which remains inconsistent. Until mandatory 2FA at publish time is enforced across all packages above a download threshold, this attack surface remains open.
body_html:
Security researchers have uncovered a coordinated supply chain attack targeting multiple popular npm packages. Malicious versions were published to the registry and downloaded thousands of times before detection.

What Was Compromised

The attack affected packages with millions of weekly downloads. The malicious code injected a data exfiltration payload that collected environment variables — including API keys, tokens, and credentials — and sent them to an attacker-controlled server.

How It Happened

Investigators believe the attackers used a combination of phishing campaigns targeting package maintainers and credential stuffing attacks against npm accounts without two-factor authentication enabled. Once inside, they published patch-version updates to avoid triggering automated review checks.

Detection and Response

npm's security team removed the malicious packages within hours of a researcher's report. Affected organizations are advised to rotate all credentials stored in CI/CD environments, audit their dependency trees, and review recent build logs for suspicious outbound connections.

The Bigger Picture

This incident is the latest in a series of npm supply chain attacks that have exposed systemic weaknesses in open-source package management. Security experts are calling for mandatory 2FA for all maintainers of high-impact packages and a more rigorous publication review process.
